summary: Jeff Atwood argues that incremental search is vastly superior to traditional find dialogs and should be standard in every application. He details the numerous usability problems with modal search dialogs—they obscure content, provide poor feedback, force users to commit to searches before seeing results, and require thinking about search direction. Drawing on Jef Raskin's 'The Human Interface,' Atwood demonstrates how incremental search solves all these problems by providing immediate feedback, allowing real-time adjustment, and staying out of the user's way. He concludes that once developers experience incremental search, traditional dialogs become unbearable.
key_points: Traditional find dialogs are fundamentally flawed: they're modal, provide no feedback until search completes, and obscure the content you're searching
Incremental search provides immediate feedback with each keystroke, allowing users to stop typing as soon as they find a match or correct mistakes instantly
The lack of dialogs in incremental search means minimal interruption to workflow—search, find, and resume working seamlessly
Users become so accustomed to bad interfaces that they 'no longer feel the pain' of poor design choices
Interface design principles strongly favor incremental search in almost all scenarios
money_quotes: Incremental search is so good that it makes traditional search dialogs completely obsolete.
This traditional method is rather punishing to the user, although most computer aficionados have become so accustomed to it that they no longer feel the pain.
If it isn't incremental, it's excremental.
You'll probably wonder why incremental search isn't included as a standard feature in every single Windows application.
The advantages of incremental searches are so numerous and the advantages of delimited search so few that I can see almost no occasions when a delimited search would be preferred.
Immediate search feedback allows you to adjust your search strategy in real time.
key_insight: Good interface design provides immediate, continuous feedback rather than forcing users into commit-then-wait cycles that obscure the connection between action and result.
